Timekeeping
You can adjust the Home Time city date and time in the Timekeeping Mode.
• The analog time of this watch is synchronized with the digital time. The analog time
setting is adjusted automatically whenever you change the digital time.
• If the analog time does not match the digital time for any reason, use the procedure
described under “To adjust home positions” (page E-42) to match the analog
setting to the digital setting.
• Pressing ' in the Timekeeping Mode toggles digital display between the Home
City Date and Home City Time.

Daylight Saving Time (DST)
Daylight Saving Time (summer time) advances the time setting by one hour from
Standard Time. Remember that not all countries or even local areas use Daylight
Saving Time.
To change the Daylight Saving Time (summer time) setting
1. In the Timekeeping Mode, hold down $ for about three
seconds until the currently selected city code ﬂ  ashes in
the upper digital display. This is the setting mode.
2. Press &. This will display the DST setting screen.
3. Use ' to toggle the DST setting on (on) and off (OFF).
• The default DST setting is off ( OFF).
4. When the setting you want is selected, press $ to exit

ing mode.

E-13
12-hour and 24-hour timekeeping
• With the 12-hour format, the PM indicator (P) appears for times in the range of noon
to 11:59 p.m. and no indicator appears for times in the range of midnight to 11:59
a.m.
• With the 24-hour format, times are displayed in the range of 0:00 to 23:59, without
the PM indicator (P).
• The 12-hour/24-hour timekeeping format you select in the Timekeeping Mode is
applied in all other modes.
E-14
Stopwatch
You can use the stopwatch to measure elapsed time, lap
times and split times. If you specify a distance value, the
stopwatch also will calculate and display speeds.
• The digital display range of the stopwatch is 99 hours,
59 minutes, 59.999 seconds. The stopwatch continues
to run until you stop it. If it reaches the above limit, it will
restart the time measurement from zero.
• The stopwatch operation continues even if you exit the
Stopwatch Mode. If you exit the Stopwatch Mode while
a stopwatch operation is in progress, the segments
along the top of the upper digital display (upper display
segments) will indicate the seconds of the current time.
Each segment represents one second. This is to let you
know that a stopwatch operation is in progress.

E-15
Important!
• Check to make sure that the speed hand is not moving before you perform any
Stopwatch Mode button operation.
Specifying a Distance Value
You can use the procedure below to specify a distance value, if you want. If you
specify a distance value, the watch will calculate and indicate speed for certain
stopwatch operations.
• The distance value represents kilometers, miles, or any other distance unit you
want. Specifying a distance value of 10.0, for example, can mean 10.0 kilometers
or 10.0 miles.
• No speed calculation is performed when 0.0 is speciﬁ  ed as the current distance
value.
• For lap time readings, you can specify either the lap distance (when all the laps are
the same distance) or the total race distance.
• For split time readings, you can specify the total race distance only. Though the
watch will indicate a speed each time you take a split reading, they will be based
on the total race distance, and so they will not indicate actual intermediate split
speeds.

Time Measurement
You need to specify either lap time (page E-20) or split time (page E-22) measurement
before you start a stopwatch operation.
•If you want to measure total elapsed time, select split time measurement.
To specify lap time or split time readings
1. In the Stopwatch Mode, make sure the stopwatch is stopped and reset to all zeros.
• Make sure that LAP (lap times) or SPL (split times) is shown in the lower digital
display.
• If you need to reset the stopwatch to all zeros, press $.
2. You can toggle between LAP (lap times) and SPL (split times) by pressing $.

Use the following procedure to measure elapsed time, without taking lap time or split
time readings.
To perform elapsed time measurement
Select split time (SPL) measurement and then perform the following button operations.
' Start ➝ ' Stop ➝ $ Reset

restart from the point where you left off.

Lap Time
A lap time reading shows the time elapsed since the last
lap time reading was taken.
•If you want the watch to calculate the speed of each lap
(when all of the laps are the same distance), specify the
lap distance for the distance setting (page E-15).
•If you want the watch to calculate the speed of the
entire race, specify the race distance for the distance
setting (page E-15). A speed will be indicated each
time you take a lap reading, but these lap speeds will
not indicate actual speed of the entire race.
To set up the stopwatch to take lap time readings
1. In the Stopwatch Mode, make sure the stopwatch is stopped and reset to all zeros.
• If you need to reset the stopwatch to all zeros, press $.
2. Make sure that LAP (lap times) is shown in the lower digital display.
• If SPL (split times) is displayed, press $ to toggle it to LAP.

Countdown Timer
You can set the countdown start time within a range of
one minute to 24 hours. An alarm sounds when the
countdown reaches zero.
• You also can select auto-repeat, which automatically
restarts the countdown from the original value you set
whenever zero is reached.
• All of the operations in this section are performed in the
Countdown Timer Mode, which you enter by pressing
& (page E-7).

E-26
To use the countdown timer
Press ' while in the Countdown Timer Mode to start the countdown timer.
• When the end of the countdown is reached, the alarm will sound for 10 seconds or
until you stop it by pressing any button.
• The countdown time is reset to its starting value automatically after the alarm stops
if auto-repeat is turned off.
• When auto-repeat is turned on, the countdown will restart automatically without
pausing when it reaches zero.
• The countdown timer measurement operation continues even if you exit the
Countdown Timer Mode.
• Press ' while a countdown operation is in progress to pause it.
• Press ' again to resume the countdown.
• To stop a countdown timer operation completely, ﬁ  rst pause it (by pressing '),
and then press $. This returns the countdown time to its starting value.

E-29
World Time
World Time digitally displays the current time in one of
48 cities (29 time zones) around the world. A simple
operation swaps your Home City with the currently
selected World Time City.
• The times kept in the World Time Mode are
synchronized with the time being kept in the
Timekeeping Mode. If you feel that there is an error in
any World Time Mode time, check to make sure you
have the correct city selected as your Home City. Also
check to make sure that the current time as shown in
the Timekeeping Mode is correct.
• Times in the World Time Mode are based on UTC
offsets. See the “City Code Table” at the back of this
manual for information about the UTC offsets that are
supported.
• All of the operations in this section are performed in the World Time Mode, which
you enter by pressing & (page E-7).

E-32
Swapping your Home City and World Time City
You can use the procedure below to swap your Home City and World Time City. This
changes your Home City to your World Time City, and your World Time City to your
Home City. This capability can come in handy when you frequently travel between two
cities in different time zones.
To swap your Home City and World Time City
1. In the World Time Mode, use ' to select the World Time City you want.
2. Hold down $ and % until the watch beeps.
• This will make the World Time City you selected in step 1 your Home City, and
cause the hour and minute hands to move to the current time in that city. At the
same time, it will change the Home City you had selected prior to step 2 your World
Time City, and cause the content of lower digital display to change accordingly.
• After swapping the Home City and World Time City, the watch stays in the World
Time Mode with the city that was selected as the Home City prior to step 2 now
displayed as the World Time City.

Alarms
When an alarm is turned on, the alarm tone will sound
when its alarm time is reached. One of the alarms is a
snooze alarm, while the other four are daily alarms.
You also can turn on an Hourly Time Signal that causes
the watch to beep twice every hour on the hour.
• There are ﬁ  ve alarm screens numbered AL1, AL2, AL3
and AL4 for the daily alarm, and a snooze alarm screen
indicated by SNZ. The Hourly Time Signal screen is
indicated by SIG.

watch is in. In the case of the snooze alarm, the alarm operation is performed a total
of seven times, every ﬁ  ve minutes, or until you turn the alarm off (page E-36).
• Alarm and Hourly Time Signal operations are performed in accordance with the
current time in your Home City, as kept in the Timekeeping Mode.

E-38
Illumination
An LED (light-emitting diode) illuminates the face of the
watch for easy reading in the dark. The watch’s auto light
switch turns on illumination automatically when you angle
the watch towards your face.
• The auto light switch must be turned on (indicated by
the auto light switch on indicator) for it to operate.
• See “Illumination Precautions” (page E-46) for other
important information about using illumination.
To illuminate the display manually
In any mode, press % to turn on illumination.
• The above operation turns on illumination regardless of
the current auto light switch setting.
• You can use the procedure below to select either 1.5
seconds or 3 seconds as the illumination duration.
When you press %, illumination will remain on for about
1.5 seconds or 3 seconds, depending on the current
illumination duration setting.

BRIEF TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS
INTRODUCTION
The Secure HHPC is a Handheld Radio set for secure mode of voice communication in VHF band along with short data messages. It is digital protocol radio with built in encryption card for high end secrecy requirements. The Radio set operates in the frequency band of 136 MHz to 174 MHz  at 12.5 kHz channel spacing in secure DMR mode and at 25 kHz channel spacing in analog FM mode(in admin mode). The Preset channels and functions are selectable with rotary switches. The radio functions are set with the alphanumeric keypad on the front panel and also configurable via a Customer Programming Software (CPS). Two different power outputs viz. high and low are selectable through the front panel and CPS channel wise. It is capable of providing line of sight (LOS) communication in clear and secure mode along with short data messages. The battery operated radio is compatible with legacy radio in clear FM wideband mode and complies ETSI standard for DMR protocol. Analog communication is available in password protected admin mode.

RADIO FEATURES
The following features are available in Secure HHPC radio.
HHPC Remote Monitor:
Remote monitor feature allows a remote user to activate a target (misplaced / lost) HHPC’s microphone and transmitter for a period of time to assist in accessing the communication transpiring on lost/misplaced handsets.
HHPC Late Entry:
HHPC allows late entry call support in DMR mode.
HHPC Remote Enable/ Disable:
Remote Disable/Enable feature to stop any inappropriate use of HHPC or to prevent a stolen HHPC from functioning.
HHPC Emergency call:
Emergency call (short duration high power transmission) for sending notification to personnel at site   to convey message with respect to any crisis situation.

prevent a stolen HHPC from functioning.
HHPC Emergency call:
Emergency call (short duration high power transmission) for sending notification to personnel at site   to convey message with respect to any crisis situation.
HHPC call types:
Type of calls: Individual, group and broadcast call facility is available.
HHPC data messaging
HHPC has facility of SMS texting in DMR mode.
Encryption:
HHPC has provision for SAG integration graded algorithm/encryption module for providing supplementary encryption.
Lone Worker Mode:
The DMR allows user to carry out automatic periodic check on personnel work alone at site.
Man-down feature:
The HHPC has special motion sensors to detect man down positions of an individual.
GPS tracker:
The HHPC Radio has inbuilt GPS tracker with 10m accuracy for better fleet management. The HHPC has feature of asking for GPS location from another HHPC and responding to this request with location coordinates.
MMI display:
The HHPC Radio has LCD with standard keypad. It displays GPS coordinates, battery strength indicator, caller ID, channel numbers etc.
Scanning on priority / Emergency call:
HHPC has a feature to automatically scan each channel in-turn and alert the user on active channel.
Fill Gun Interface:
The Radio Set receives the secure key data from Fill Gun when the Fill Gun is connected to the Radio Set and the “LOAD” key of the Fill Gun is pressed. This facility is password protected.

Remote Kill:

Functionality wise, on the transmit path, the radio receives clear voice from mic input (internal/external) through Codec. Data is processed in the required format. In order to initiate the communication in DMR clear mode or DMR custom mode, the controller checks for the presence of the encryption module PCB. In case the handshake is successful, the call/message is initiated. The custom channel is configured only after handshake. For analog wideband mode communication, the user should first go to admin mode. Then, the communication is possible in analog wideband mode only if the handshake with encryption module PCB is successful, i.e. after checking the presence of encryption module PCB. In custom encryption mode, the voice/data is passed on to the crypto card for encryption. In clear mode, data is passed onto the encryption module PCB and then modulated using TX up-converter IC and is sent to Power Amplifier card for further transmission. For SMS, the data is transmitted instead of voice in a similar manner. The clear analog wideband communication is plain FM communication in the range of 136-174MHz.

Then this data is passed onto the DMR processor for audio level conversion. Codec is also used in this path. The details of major modules of the system are as follows:-
1. Baseband card:
The battery mating connector, directly mounted on the baseband card received +7.4V (nom) from battery. The baseband card contains DC-DC converters for converting 7.4V into DC power supplies essential for operating all the modules. First U24 IC converts the incoming supply from battery (7.5V nominal) into 5V and 3.6V. These supplies in turn are converted into various supplies used for powering the analog and digital sections of the PCB. The digital section consists of Controller and processor both programmed for functions related to radio functionality. Keypad along with backlit graphic LCD display provides a user interface to crypto product operator for MMI operations. The detachable encryption module holds the algorithm for custom encryption. The baseband card also contains relevant interface ICs for specific radio functions like GPS location information, man-down feature implementation. The encryption module is an essential component for all types of communication. In analog wide-band mode (25 kHz channel spacing), the presence of encryption module is checked through handshake by the controller present on baseband card on I2C interface. In case of DMR clear mode also, the data is passed onto the encryption module for communication, through no encryption is done.

le is checked through handshake by the controller present on baseband card on I2C interface. In case of DMR clear mode also, the data is passed onto the encryption module for communication, through no encryption is done.
2. Power Amplifier Card: The Power amplifier module has major functions. First, in receive mode, i.e. when PTT is not pressed, it accepts the receive signal, does harmonic filtering, and amplifies it using low noise amplifier (LNA) for onward processing at baseband card. Secondly, in Tx mode, when PTT is pressed, it amplifies the transmit signal received from BB card to required level, i.e. 5W in high power mode and 1W in low power mode. Thirdly, it extracts the GPS signal from the receive signal and sends to Base Band card for extracting GPS information required by the controller.
3. Crypto card: The Crypto card provides the data encryption/decryption function of the equipment. This crypto card is physically mounted as a detachable module on the base band module. The Clear data from controller is encrypted in the crypto card and then passed on to the network controller of the baseband card. The crypto module interfaces with the baseband module of HHPC and it is a add on module to radio. The crypto module is a FPGA based high performance card. This card supports I2C protocol for data communication and UART protocol for key and algorithm loading. Module contains SPI Flash, RTC, and Schmitt trigger based RNG circuit.

The microcontroller used is a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 based microcontrollers with Floating Point Unit (FPU) for embedded applications which include an ARM Cortex-M0 coprocessor, with 1 MB of flash and 136 kB of on-chip SRAM and 16 kB of EEPROM memory. This MCU operates at a 12.28 MHz clock and 3.3V DC.
The controller contains code for controlling all the interfaces, including taking user inputs via keypad and displaying status to user using display. It also connects to crypto card using I2C interface. It also interfaces with function specific ICs for GPS and man down feature implementation.
b) DMR Processor
The DMR processor is responsible for analog mode and digital protocol implementation. The DSP circuit consists of a DSP and processes the base band signal. The DSP operates on an external clock of 12.28 MHz . The I/O section operates at 3.3V and the core section operates at 1.2V. It is a low power high performance base band processor supporting DMR protocol. With an external codec (audio band ADC and DAC), it completes the entire physical layer and data link layer, and most of the call control layer processing of DMR protocol. In DMR mode, it complies with ETSI TS 102 361.  This processor carries out the following processes:
Digital processing
• 4Level FSK and Baseband filter processing
• Vo-coder processing between audio codec and modulation/ demodulation

It is also responsible for Pre-emphasis/De-emphasis, Compressor/Expander processing and DMR protocol implementation.
Power Amplifier Card:
(Refer BD 1713 008 375 52)
The Power amplifier module has major functions. First, in receive mode, i.e. when PTT is not pressed, it accepts the receive signal, does harmonic filtering, and amplifies it using low noise amplifier (LNA) for onward processing at baseband card. Secondly, in Tx mode, when PTT is pressed, it amplifies the transmit signal received from BB card to required level, i.e. 5W in high power mode and 1W in low power mode. Thirdly, it extracts the GPS signal from the receive signal and sends to Base Band card for extracting GPS information required by the controller.

3. Crypto card: The block diagram of crypto card is shown in Figure 1.8.4. The Crypto card provides the data encryption/decryption function of the equipment. This crypto card is physically mounted as a detachable module on the base band module. The Clear data from controller is encrypted in the crypto card and then passed on to the network controller of the baseband card. 1.1	GENERAL PRECAUTIONS
The Secure Handheld Radio is modular in construction. Each Module sits on guided rivet on chassis and polarized Female connector, so the complete subsystem can be assembled/ disassembled easily. The socket, connectors and other items have generally been so designed with polarized connection so no interchange is possible. The disconnected items such as wires etc. should be properly tagged / marked with identification mark to enable easy identification. The module should be tightened on chassis only after the proper mating with rivet spacers /Guides and respective mating connectors.
The ICs, discrete components, and other components used in the Secure Handheld Radio, require careful handling. Care should be taken while replacing these components and only a miniature soldering iron of 25 watts should be used for soldering. A high standard of soldering should be maintained. Wiring of the circuit should be fiddled as minimum as possible.

DMR CPS (Digital Mobile Radio Customer Programming Software) is a software application used to configure and program DMR device. It allows users to set up radio parameters, channels, contacts, and features according to their operational needs. This Customer Programing Software has multiple tabs: General, Channel Configuration, Misc Features, and Contact Lists.  When CPS software is opened, it shows its login screen as follows. Enter the provided username and password.
General –
In general Tab there are following features:
Serial Port Section includes COM Port selection and Baud rate (i.e. 38400). Select the COM port where the programming cable of radio is connected.
Radio Information Section includes Radio name and Radio ID. Password Section includes Admin Password and Power on Password. This section sets the Power ON password and the Admin password of the radio.
Side Key Programming Section includes Side Key 1 and Side Key 2. Both key is configurable and options available is Power, Squelch, Backlight and Power Save. If we select Power, the key pressing toggles the output RF power between Low Power and High Power. Squelch option is available only in Analog Mode.

FAULT DIAGNOSIS
3.0 	GENERAL
The Radio set being modular in construction, fault can be easily traced and pin pointed by isolating the faulty module whenever the set fails to operate. The repair of a faulty set can be performed only with good background knowledge of the working of the set.
Whenever the Receiver Transmitter develops a fault, the troubleshooting procedure given below may be followed. Many symptoms are discussed along with probable faulty module/component. If a module is suspected it can be tested, before replacing it with a Spare module. The objective is to make sure that the module in question is indeed faulty before replacing and to avoid arbitrary replacement of modules. The faulty module can be then thoroughly checked by referring to the Technical Mechanical Part III Overhaul and Reconditioning Instructions.
3.1     TROUBLESHOOTING
The following procedure will enable to quick the effective repair of  Secure HHPC , to identify the faulty module. These methods provide an elementary fault finding procedure. It is not exhaustive and may be used as broad guidelines only.
3.1.1   TROUBLESHOOTING WITH THE HELP OF FLOWCHARTS
1. The troubleshooting has been covered systematically system wise and then card/Module wise. The following layout for the fault diagnosis is to be followed.
Procedure-1:  Dead unit troubleshooting flowchart : This procedure is to followed when Radio Set is dead.
Procedure-2: BITE check : This procedure is to check the failure of any module in BITE.
